chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: Changes Around Us: Physical and Chemical

points: A physical change alters only the physical properties (shape, size, state) of a substance without forming any new substance — examples: melting ice, folding paper, boiling water, crushing chalk.

A chemical change produces one or more new substances through a chemical reaction — examples: rusting of iron, combustion, curdling of milk, mixing vinegar with baking soda (produces CO₂); chemical changes are represented by chemical equations.
Some processes involve both physical and chemical changes simultaneously — e.g., burning a candle (melting and evaporation of wax are physical; combustion of wax vapour is chemical).

summary.category: Combustion, Fire Triangle, and Key Chemical Reactions
points: Combustion is a chemical reaction in which a substance (combustible/fuel) reacts with oxygen to produce heat and/or light; the minimum temperature required for a substance to catch fire is its ignition temperature.
Three conditions are essential for combustion (Fire Triangle): a combustible substance, oxygen, and heat to reach the ignition temperature — removing any one stops the fire.

CO₂ turns lime water milky (forming white calcium carbonate) — this is the standard test for carbon dioxide; CO₂ is produced when exhaled air is blown into lime water and when vinegar reacts with baking soda.

summary.category: Classification of Changes and Natural Processes
points: Changes can be reversible (original substance recoverable — e.g., melting ice, boiling water) or irreversible (original cannot be recovered — e.g., burning wood, making popcorn, chopping vegetables).
Changes can also be desirable (useful — e.g., ripening of fruits, making curd, composting) or undesirable (harmful — e.g., rusting of iron, food decay, atmospheric pollution from burning fuels).

Rocks undergo weathering (physical and chemical breakdown by temperature, water, roots, and chemical reactions) and erosion (transport of rock particles by wind and water), both of which lead to soil formation over thousands of years.
